meta.title: The Frontier Macro
meta.subtitle: Rates, FX, the External Sector & Global Markets
meta.forAudience: For Quick Reference Only - Bangladesh & Global Markets
meta.headline: Bangladesh’s external sector presented a mixed picture during the first eleven months of FY26. The current account deficit narrowed sharply to USD 301 million from USD 1.23 billion in the corresponding period of the previous fiscal year, primarily driven by record-high remittance inflows. In contrast, the trade deficit widened to USD 23.98 billion from USD 19.38 billion as imports rose 6.3% year-on-year to USD 64.02 billion, while exports declined 2.0% to USD 40.04 billion. Encouragingly, the financial account recorded a notable recovery, posting a surplus of USD 4.16 billion compared with a deficit of USD 214 million during the same period of FY25.

commentary.fx: Imports increased 5.9% year-on-year to USD 67.7 billion during the period, reflecting stronger domestic demand. However, import growth remained below the pace of remittance growth. Meanwhile, exports rose by over 11% to USD 44.2 billion.
commentary.global: A high-level Bangladeshi delegation will travel to the United States next week to seek support from the 54 member states of the UN Economic and Social Council (ECOSOC) for Bangladesh's request to defer its graduation from Least Developed Country (LDC) status by three years.
commentary.rates: The Bangladesh Bank has revised down its private sector credit growth target to 6.8% for December 2026 under the Monetary Policy Statement (MPS) for the first half of FY2026-27.
commentary.ycurve: Finance Minister Amir Khosru Mahmud Chowdhury assured that depositors of the country's five troubled banks will receive 100% of their deposits along with accrued interest, ruling out any possibility of a deposit haircut.
commentary.inflation: Consumer lending continued its upward trend in the January–March quarter of FY26, with outstanding loans exceeding BDT 1.58 trillion, despite elevated lending rates and persistent inflationary pressures.
commentary.external: Bangladesh's current account deficit (CAD) narrowed significantly to USD 301 million during July–May FY26, compared with USD 1.23 billion in the corresponding period of the previous fiscal year, supported by record remittance inflows and moderate import growth.
commentary.reserves: Bangladesh's financial account posted a surplus of USD 4.16 billion during the first 11 months of FY26, marking a sharp turnaround from a USD 214 million deficit in the same period of FY25, according to Bangladesh Bank.
commentary.commodity: Global oil prices surged more than 5% after US President Donald Trump stated that the memorandum of understanding to end the conflict with Iran was "over," raising concerns over potential disruptions to Middle East oil supplies. Brent crude rose to USD 77.98 per barrel (+5.15%), while WTI crude climbed to USD 74.14 per barrel (+5.25%), the highest levels since 23 June.

fx.text: Imports increased 5.9% year-on-year to USD 67.7 billion during the period, reflecting stronger domestic demand. However, import growth remained below the pace of remittance growth. Meanwhile, exports rose by over 11% to USD 44.2 billion.
The taka was unchanged at 122.85 per USD (interbank), with the BC sell rate at 123.75.

global.text: A high-level Bangladeshi delegation will travel to the United States next week to seek support from the 54 member states of the UN Economic and Social Council (ECOSOC) for Bangladesh's request to defer its graduation from Least Developed Country (LDC) status by three years.
Term SOFR spans 3.76-4.05% across tenors, while EURIBOR sits lower at 2.35%+.
The 3-month SOFR-EURIBOR gap is 1.41pp, a live input for currency-of-borrowing decisions.

rates.text: The Bangladesh Bank has revised down its private sector credit growth target to 6.8% for December 2026 under the Monetary Policy Statement (MPS) for the first half of FY2026-27.
The repo rate is 10.00%, the Bank Rate 4.00%.

ycurve.text: Finance Minister Amir Khosru Mahmud Chowdhury assured that depositors of the country's five troubled banks will receive 100% of their deposits along with accrued interest, ruling out any possibility of a deposit haircut.
The local-currency curve is upward-sloping: the 91-day (9.33%) sits below the 10-year (10.24%).
Week-on-week the curve shifted down about 0.15pp on average.

inflation.text: Consumer lending continued its upward trend in the January–March quarter of FY26, with outstanding loans exceeding BDT 1.58 trillion, despite elevated lending rates and persistent inflationary pressures.

external.text: Bangladesh's current account deficit (CAD) narrowed significantly to USD 301 million during July–May FY26, compared with USD 1.23 billion in the corresponding period of the previous fiscal year, supported by record remittance inflows and moderate import growth.
Exports were $4.20bn against imports of $5.80bn - a trade gap of $1.60bn.

reserves.text: Bangladesh's financial account posted a surplus of USD 4.16 billion during the first 11 months of FY26, marking a sharp turnaround from a USD 214 million deficit in the same period of FY25, according to Bangladesh Bank.
Gross reserves was little changed at $36.08bn (BPM6 $31.53bn).

commodity.text: Global oil prices surged more than 5% after US President Donald Trump stated that the memorandum of understanding to end the conflict with Iran was "over," raising concerns over potential disruptions to Middle East oil supplies. Brent crude rose to USD 77.98 per barrel (+5.15%), while WTI crude climbed to USD 74.14 per barrel (+5.25%), the highest levels since 23 June.

Bangladesh Bank - month-end gross & BPM6 reserves; FY26 remittance total
EPB - June & FY26 export earnings and the trade balance

special.title: Bangladesh Bank Monetary Policy Statement (H1 FY27) — Summary
points: Policy rate held at 10.0% (unchanged since Oct 2024); SLF at 11.5%, SDF at 7.5% — contractionary stance continues into H1FY27
Inflation: eased from 11.7% (Jul 2024) to 9.4% (May 2026); projected to moderate to ~8.9% by Dec 2026
Growth: FY26 GDP growth ~4.14%; FY27 target 6.5% (BB forecasts 6.1%)
Tk. 60,000 crore stimulus package for industry, agriculture & CMSMEs — expected to create ~25 lakh jobs
Private sector credit growth slowed to 5.5% (Jun 2026); projected to recover to 8.0% by Jun 2027
NPLs remain elevated: gross NPL ratio at 32.26% (Mar 2026); new Bank Resolution Act & Deposit Protection Act 2026 introduced
External sector strengthened: reserves rose to $31.74B; remittances up 19.07%; exchange rate stable (~BDT 123/USD)
Key risks: Middle East conflict, energy/fertilizer price shocks, high NPLs, weak private investment
